text: The surgeon inserts a lubricated disposable SILS port for single-port laparoscopy, creating a pneumo-rectum to provide space for dissection. The dissection begins four centimeters from the anal verge, progressing cephalad with a combination of sharp and blunt techniques. The limited space initially complicates the procedure, but as dissection continues, more space becomes available, allowing the lesion to be fully mobilized and released while maintaining adequate margins.

After excising the rectal tumor, the surgeon leaves the extraperitoneal defect open following hemostasis and wound revision. The SILS port is removed, and the tumor is collected. The patient is repositioned in a modified Light-Davis position for the laparoscopic abdominal approach. The surgeon mobilizes the left colon and splenic flexure from medial to lateral. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified, isolated, and ligated using ligature blunt tip forceps, which are also used to seal the inferior mesenteric artery. This dissection creates a window to access Toad's fascia, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon opens the pre-sacral plane and identifies the tattooed segment of the colon. The upper rectum is skeletonized, and the recto-sigma junction is stapled using an endo-GIA stapler. The left lateral gutter is fully mobilized. A peri-umbilical incision of approximately four centimeters is made for specimen retrieval and the introduction of the circular stapler anvil. The procedure concludes with incisional closure and the performance of an end-to-end anastomosis.
